topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the room was thick, suffocating. I found it when I opened my desk drawer, intending to grab a pen, and saw it—my journal, spine cracked open, the pages filled with my most private anxieties, meticulously annotated. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt like swallowing glass. I slowly closed the drawer, my fingers trembling so violently I had to grip the wood of the desk. When my roommate, Alex, walked in, humming some off-key tune, I kept my gaze fixed on the floor, trying to project an image of calm I absolutely did not feel. "Everything okay?" Alex asked, their tone light, almost breezy. I managed a tight, strained smile. "Yeah, fine," I mumbled, the sound barely audible. My chest felt tight, a knot tightening with every beat of my heart. I just wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 2]

It was the discovery that hit me like a physical blow. I was sorting through my textbooks, needing a distraction, when I noticed the slight misalignment of the shelf where my journal usually sat. Curiosity, a dangerous impulse, pulled me closer. The sight of my handwriting, my whispered fears laid bare in ink, sent a wave of profound distress washing over me. I felt my cheeks flush hot, and a tremor ran through my hands, making the pages feel slippery. When Alex entered, carrying a stack of laundry, I quickly tried to cover the book with my arm, a futile gesture. "Hey," Alex said, setting the clothes down. "What are you doing?" My voice came out thin, reedy. I just stared at the floor, unable to meet their eyes, the feeling in my stomach churning with a heavy, unwelcome dread.

[story 3]

A cold, hollow sensation settled deep in my core. I found the evidence tucked beneath a loose floorboard—my diary, pages turned, secrets exposed. The air in the cramped room seemed to thin out, leaving me feeling strangely lightheaded. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up, rigid with a desperate need to flee. When Alex finally appeared, looking for a charger, I didn't look up. I just stood there, my shoulders hunched high around my ears, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. "Did you find anything interesting?" Alex inquired, their voice carrying a note of casual inquiry that grated on my nerves. I shook my head once, a jerky, small motion, and turned abruptly, walking toward the door as if escaping a sudden, invisible fire.
